title: Differential testing — interpreter vs compiled equivalence
purpose: JIT compilers are notoriously hard to test. The interpreter is the correctness oracle — it is correct by construction (verified across Phases 0-3 with 1020+ tests). Differential testing runs every test through both interpreter and JIT/AOT paths and compares results. Any divergence is a compiler bug. Per D11: differential testing is the primary correctness strategy. Property-based testing and fuzzing complement this by generating inputs the test suite doesn't cover.
task: Implement a differential test runner in crates/beamr/tests/differential.rs that executes functions through both interpreter and compiled paths and compares results. Implement a property-based test generator that creates random BEAM programs (valid instruction sequences) and verifies interpreter/compiler equivalence. The differential runner is an integration test harness, not a unit test — it exercises the full pipeline (load, compile, execute, compare).

requirements.title: Differential test runner
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a DifferentialRunner that accepts a BEAM function (module bytes or instruction sequence) and executes it through both the interpreter and the JIT compiler, comparing the results. WHEN both paths produce the same result, THE SYSTEM SHALL report success. WHEN the results diverge, THE SYSTEM SHALL report a DivergenceError containing the function identity, input arguments, interpreter result, and compiled result.
acceptance: DifferentialRunner::new(config) creates a runner with a scheduler config (threshold=1 to force immediate compilation)
DifferentialRunner::run(module_bytes, function, arity, args) -> DifferentialResult
DifferentialResult::Match { result: Term } when both paths agree
DifferentialResult::Divergence { function, args, interpreted, compiled } when paths disagree
DifferentialResult::CompilationSkipped { reason } when JIT cannot compile the function
Test: fibonacci(10) -> Match { result: 55 }
Test: intentionally broken compiled path (mock) -> Divergence reported

requirements.title: Result comparison with Term equality
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL compare interpreter and compiled results using structural Term equality (the existing PartialEq implementation). WHEN the result is an exception, THE SYSTEM SHALL compare exception class, reason, and stacktrace MFAs (ignoring stacktrace line numbers since compiled frames have none per B-137 R5). WHEN the result involves heap-allocated terms (tuples, lists, binaries), THE SYSTEM SHALL perform deep structural comparison, not pointer comparison.
acceptance: compare_results(interpreted, compiled) -> CompareResult::Equal or CompareResult::Different { diff }
Integer, atom, and pid results compared by value
Tuple results compared element-by-element recursively
List results compared element-by-element recursively
Exception results compared by class + reason (stacktrace MFAs only, no line numbers)
Test: identical deep tuples -> Equal
Test: different nested list element -> Different with path to divergence

requirements.title: Divergence reporting with reproduction information
requirements.spec: WHEN a divergence is detected, THE SYSTEM SHALL produce a DivergenceReport containing: (1) the function MFA, (2) the input arguments as formatted Terms, (3) the interpreter result, (4) the compiled result, (5) the BEAM bytecode of the function (for reproduction). THE SYSTEM SHALL format the report as a human-readable string suitable for test failure output. THE SYSTEM SHALL also write the diverging function's bytecode to a file for offline reproduction.
acceptance: DivergenceReport has fields: mfa, args, interpreted_result, compiled_result, bytecode
DivergenceReport::to_string() produces a human-readable diff
DivergenceReport::save_bytecode(path) writes the function bytecode to a file
The report includes enough information to reproduce the divergence outside the test harness
Test: divergence report for {ok, 42} vs {ok, 43} shows the differing element

requirements.title: Test suite integration — run existing tests differentially
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a macro or test helper that wraps existing unit test functions to run them differentially. WHEN applied to an existing test, THE SYSTEM SHALL execute the test function through both interpreter and compiled paths and assert equivalence. THE SYSTEM SHALL skip differential execution for tests that use features not yet supported by the JIT (maps, binaries, float arithmetic) and log the skip reason.
acceptance: differential_test!(module_bytes, function, arity, args) macro runs both paths and asserts Match
Unsupported-opcode functions are skipped with CompilationSkipped, not failed
Skip reasons are logged (not silently swallowed) so coverage gaps are visible
Test: existing fibonacci test wrapped in differential_test! passes
Test: function with map operations -> CompilationSkipped logged

requirements.title: Property-based test generator — random BEAM programs
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a proptest generator that produces random valid BEAM instruction sequences covering the JIT-supported opcode subset (arithmetic, comparison, control flow, type tests, tuple/list operations, try/catch). WHEN a generated program causes a divergence, THE SYSTEM SHALL shrink the failing case to the minimal reproducing program. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT generate opcodes outside the JIT-supported set (no maps, binaries, float ops, receive).
acceptance: BeamProgramStrategy generates random functions with 5-50 instructions

Generated instructions are drawn from: move, swap, add, sub, mul, is_integer, is_atom, is_tuple, put_tuple2, put_list, get_tuple_element, get_hd, get_tl, call, return, try, try_end, try_case, test_arity, select_val, jump, label
Generated programs are syntactically valid (labels resolve, registers in range, arities consistent)
proptest shrinking produces minimal failing cases (fewer instructions, smaller values)
Test: run 1000 generated programs differentially — all Match or CompilationSkipped (no Divergence on correct JIT)

requirements.title: Side-effect equivalence checking
requirements.spec: WHEN the test function produces side effects (process dictionary writes, message sends, exception raises), THE SYSTEM SHALL compare the side-effect traces between interpreter and compiled execution. THE SYSTEM SHALL capture: messages sent (destination pid + message term), process dictionary modifications (key + value), and exceptions raised (class + reason). THE SYSTEM SHALL normalise pid values before comparison — separate executions produce different pids, so pid comparison uses ordinal position (first spawned pid, second spawned pid) rather than raw pid values. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT compare timing-dependent side effects (timestamps, scheduling order).
acceptance: SideEffectTrace captures: messages_sent, pdict_modifications, exceptions_raised
DifferentialRunner captures side-effect traces for both execution paths
Side-effect comparison reports which side effect differs
Test: function that sends a message — both paths send the same message to the same pid
Test: function that modifies process dictionary — both paths produce same pdict state

boundaries: SHALL NOT test opcodes not yet supported by JIT (maps, binaries, float arithmetic, receive) — skip with logged reason
SHALL NOT compare timing-dependent behaviour (scheduling order, wall-clock times)
SHALL NOT test concurrent multi-process scenarios — differential testing is per-function, single-process
SHALL NOT run differential tests in the default cargo test profile — gate behind a differential cfg or integration test flag to avoid slowing the standard test suite
verification: cargo test -p beamr --test differential passes with all tests green
differential.rs is under 500 lines of production code
At least 3 existing test scenarios (fibonacci, list processing, pattern matching) run differentially
Property-based generator produces and tests at least 100 random programs per run
